coredata.dc:title: Construction of an effective ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform for specific and visual detection of mercury ions based on target-triggered the inhibition on inner filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Lei
Liu, Shi Gang
Dong, Xue Zhen
Liang, Jia Yu
Li, Nian Bing
Luo, Hong Qun
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive and selective determination of mercury ion (Hg2+) is critical for human health and environmental monitoring. Herein we construct an effective ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform by combining green fluorescent polymer carbon dots (PCDs) and red fluorescent tetraphenylporphyrin tetrasulfonic acid hydrate (TPPS) for specific and visual detection of Hg2+. The fluorescence of PCDs can be quenched by TPPS through inner filter effect (IEF). In the presence of both Mn2+ and Hg2+, however, Hg2+ can expedite the complexation of TPPS and Mn2+, which causes the decrease in both fluorescence and absorption of TPPS, accompanied by the fluorescence recovery of PCDs due to the subdued IFE between TPPS and PCDs. Based on the change of fluorescence signal, a ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform is constructed for specific and visual detection of Hg2+. The proposed approach presents a fine linear range for Hg2+ over the range of 10–200 nM with a detection limit of 0.038 nM. Moreover, an easily distinguishable fluorescence color change from pink to green with the increase of Hg2+ concentration can be observed by the naked eye under a UV lamp. Such a simple and effective method shows great potential for visual sensing of Hg2+ in on-site and resource-limited settings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mercury ion
Ratiometric fluorescence
Visual sensing
Porphyrin
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor by combining PCDs and TPPS
Principle and feasibility of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Hg2+
Optimization for ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Hg2+
Determination of Hg2+ in tap water and river water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) AFM image and (B) the corresponding height profile of PCDs. Fluorescence spectra of (C) PCDs and (D) TPPS aqueous solution. Insets show the corresponding photographs of the PCDs and TPPS solutions under daylight lamp and 365 nm UV lamp, respectively. (E) Fluorescence spectra of PCDs (a), TPPS (b), and PCDs–TPPS hybrid (c) under excitation at 410 nm. Inset displays the corresponding photographs under a 365 nm UV lamp. (F) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum of the PCDs–TPPS hybrid.

(A) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of PCDs, PCDs +0.5 μM TPPS, and PCDs +1.0 μM TPPS. (B) Observed (E obsd, a) and corrected (E cor, b) fluorescence quenching efficiency after the addition of different concentrations of TPPS to the PCDs solution. (C) Fluorescence spectra of PCDs–TPPS (a), PCDs–TPPS–Mn2+ (b), PCDs–TPPS–Hg2+ (c), and PCDs–TPPS–Mn2+–Hg2+ (d). Inset displays the corresponding fluorescent photographs under a 365 nm UV lamp. (D) Fluorescence intensity of TPPS versus reaction time after the addition of different metal ions. (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of TPPS (a), TPPS–Mn2+ (b), TPPS–Hg2+ (c), and TPPS–Mn2+–Hg2+ (d). Inset shows the magnified UV–vis absorption spectra in the wavelength range of 480–675 nm. (F) Fluorescence excitation spectrum of PCDs (a), and UV–vis absorption spectra of TPPS (b) and TPPS–Mn2+–Hg2+ (c). Experimental conditions: 40 mM BR buffer, pH 7.0; TPPS, 2 μM; Mn2+, 150 μM; Hg2+, 200 nM; λex =410 nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of PCDs in the presence of different TPPS levels. Inset is the corresponding fluorescent photographs under a 365 nm UV lamp. (B) The changes of fluorescence intensity at 508 and 643 nm of the PCDs–TPPS hybrid with the concentration of TPPS. Experimental conditions: 40 mM BR buffer, pH 7.0; λex =410 nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the sensing system (PCDs–TPPS–Mn2+) after the addition of various concentrations of Hg2+. (B) The fluorescence intensity variation of the sensing system with the concentration of Hg2+. Inset is the corresponding fluorescent photographs under a 365 nm UV lamp. (C) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 643/I 508) and the concentration of Hg2+. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of the sensing system after the addition of various levels of Hg2+. Inset: The linear relationship between the absorbance at 413 nm and the concentration of Hg2+. (E) The corresponding fluorescent photographs under a 365 nm UV lamp with the Hg2+ level ranging from 0 to 200 nM. Experimental conditions: 40 mM BR buffer, pH 7.0; TPPS, 2 μM; Mn2+, 150 μM; reaction time, 40 min; λex = 410 nm.

(A) and (B) Fluorescence spectra of the sensing system (PCDs–TPPS–Mn2+) after the addition of Hg2+ and other potential interfering ions. (C) and (D) Fluorescence responses of sensing system to these potential interfering ions. Inset displays the corresponding fluorescent photographs under a 365 nm UV lamp. Experimental conditions: 40 mM BR buffer, pH 7.0; TPPS, 2 μM; Mn2+, 150 μM; reaction time, 40 min; λex = 410 nm.
Schematic illustration of the constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform for specific and visual detection of Hg2+.

head.ce:title: Construction of an effective ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform for specific and visual detection of mercury ions based on target-triggered the inhibition on inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Tiansheng Road, BeiBei District, Chongqing, 400715, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive and selective determination of mercury ion (Hg2+) is critical for human health and environmental monitoring. Herein we construct an effective ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform by combining green fluorescent polymer carbon dots (PCDs) and red fluorescent tetraphenylporphyrin tetrasulfonic acid hydrate (TPPS) for specific and visual detection of Hg2+. The fluorescence of PCDs can be quenched by TPPS through inner filter effect (IEF). In the presence of both Mn2+ and Hg2+, however, Hg2+ can expedite the complexation of TPPS and Mn2+, which causes the decrease in both fluorescence and absorption of TPPS, accompanied by the fluorescence recovery of PCDs due to the subdued IFE between TPPS and PCDs. Based on the change of fluorescence signal, a ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform is constructed for specific and visual detection of Hg2+. The proposed approach presents a fine linear range for Hg2+ over the range of 10–200 nM with a detection limit of 0.038 nM. Moreover, an easily distinguishable fluorescence color change from pink to green with the increase of Hg2+ concentration can be observed by the naked eye under a UV lamp. Such a simple and effective method shows great potential for visual sensing of Hg2+ in on-site and resource-limited settings.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ion (Hg2+), as one of the most toxic heavy metal ions and widespread pollutants, has caused serious adverse effects on both environment and human health [1,2]. It has been suggested that Hg2+ can accumulate in the human body through the skin, respiratory, and food chain, which can further cause the disorder and damage of brain, kidney, liver, endocrine systems, and central nervous systems [3–5]. Given its high toxicity and bioaccumulation, Hg2+ has been listed as a toxic priority pollutant by the U. S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [6]. Additionally, it is very difficult to purify Hg2+-contaminated wastewater and soil because of its durability and non-biodegradability [7]. Consequently, developing a straightforward and effective approach for sensitively and selectively detecting and monitoring the content of Hg2+ is critically significant for human health and environmental monitoring.

Until now, several approaches have been developed for the determination of Hg2+, including cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry [8], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [9], resonance Rayleigh scattering [10–12], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [13], colorimetry [14–18], electrochemistry [19,20], and fluorescence assay [21–26]. Among these approaches, fluorescence assay possesses significant superiority due to its high sensitivity, simplicity, rapid response, easy visualization, and real-time analysis [27–30]. However, the majority of previously reported fluorescent approaches for Hg2+ detection is usually based on measuring the change of single fluorescence signal, which is susceptible to environmental factors and instrumental efficiency [31,32]. Compared with the conventional fluorescence assay, ratiometric fluorescence assay can achieve more sensitive and reliable detection for analyte because it can simultaneously record the change of fluorescence signals at two or more different wavelengths, which provides an intrinsic self-calibration for signal correction [33–37]. Moreover, the visual analysis can be achieved by the naked eye to observe the variation of two or more different fluorescence colors, which is extremely desirable for the detection in on-site and resource-constrained settings [38–41].

Recently, some interesting ratiometric fluorescent sensors have been reported for the determination of Hg2+ [42–52]. For example, Shamsipur’s group and Chen’s group reported nanohybrids based on carbon dots-nanocluster for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ [42,44]. Mahmoudi’s group and Xie’s group developed ratiometric probes based on carbon dots-CdTe quantum dots for sensing of Hg2+ [45,49]. Jiang’s group and Zhao’s group synthesized dual-emission carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ [46,47]. Xian’s group developed a black phosphorus quantum dots-based ratiometric fluorescence probe for the determination of Hg2+ [52]. However, the majority of these ratiometric fluorescent sensors are based on one fluorescence signal change and another fluorescence signal as a reference or a low quantum yield of the fluorescent probe, which lead to an indistinguishable fluorescence color change, greatly limiting the capability of visual analysis. In general, an ideal ratiometric fluorescent sensor should have two emission bands that do not overlap each other, and these two fluorescence signals are independently responsive to analyte. Moreover, the sensor should show an obvious fluorescence color change rather than only fluorescence brightness change for analyte [39,53]. Accordingly, it is still urgently needed and challenging to develop an effective ratiometric fluorescent sensor with a distinguishable fluorescence color change for specific and visual sensing of Hg2+.

In this work, we construct an effective ratiometric fluorescent sensor by combining the easily synthetic fluorescent polymer carbon dots (PCDs) and commercially available tetraphenylporphyrin tetrasulfonic acid hydrate (TPPS) for specific and visual detection of Hg2+. The green fluorescence of PCDs can be effectively quenched by TPPS through inner filter effect (IEF), as illustrated in Scheme 1 . The sensing system shows two well-resolved fluorescence peaks at the optimal excitation wavelength. Upon the addition of Mn2+ and Hg2+, Hg2+ can expedite the complexation of TPPS and Mn2+ to form manganese porphyrin (Mn–TPPS). The formation of Mn–TPPS causes the decrease of both fluorescence and absorption of TPPS, accompanied with the fluorescence recovery of PCDs due to the subdued IFE between the TPPS and PCDs. Meanwhile, an easily distinguishable fluorescent color change from pink to yellow to green with the increase of Hg2+ level was observed by the naked eye when illuminated with UV light. On the basis of this principle, an effective ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform has been constructed for specific and visual determination of Hg2+. Furthermore, the proposed ratiometric fluorescent sensor was also applied to determine Hg2+ in real water samples with satisfactory results.

TPPS was supplied by TCI Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Mercury nitrate (Hg(NO3)2·H2O) was acquired from Shandong Xiya Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Shandong, China). Arsenic ion (As3+) standard solution and hydroquinone were provided by Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine and other metal salts such as MnCl2·4H2O, KCl, Na3PO4·12H2O, NaNO3, CaCl2·2H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, AgNO3, BaCl2·2H2O, CoCl2·6(H2O), Pb(NO3)2, AlCl3·6H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, NiSO4·6(H2O), and Mg(NO3)2·6H2O were bought from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagents Factory (Sichuan, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was prepared with an Aquapro ultrapure water machine and used for the entire experiment.

Fluorescence spectra were obtained from a Hitachi F–2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a photomultiplier tube voltage of 400 V (Hitachi, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was taken on a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN electron microscope (FEI, USA). Fluorescence lifetime measurement was performed using an FLS 980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image was obtained from a dimension icon atomic force microscopy (Bruker, Germany). UV–vis absorption spectra were achieved on a UV–vis 2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). All photos were taken by a HUAWEI smartphone.
The fluorescent PCDs were synthesized referring to our previously reported approach [54,55]. The detailed synthesis processes are presented in the Supplementary Material.

For the ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+, 10 μL of PCDs dilute solution, 20 μL of 100 μM TPPS solution, and 15 μL of 10 mM MnCl2 solution were added to 935 μL of Britton–Robinson (BR) buffer (40 mM, pH 7.0) solution. Subsequently, the Hg2+ solutions with different levels varying from 0 to 400 nM were added to the above mixture. The mixtures were sufficiently shaken, and then incubated for 40 min under ambient conditions before the fluorescence and absorption spectra measurements. The fluorescence spectra of the sensing system were collected at an excitation wavelength of 410 nm. The selective experiments were performed by adding other inorganic ions instead of Hg2+ in the same approach as described above.

To estimate the feasibility and practicality of this approach, the detection of Hg2+ was carried out in tap water and river water samples, respectively. The two kinds of samples were collected from our laboratory and Jialing River (Chongqing, China), respectively. The obtained water samples were filtered by a 0.22 μm filtration membrane first, and were then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, the pretreated water samples were spiked with various levels of Hg2+, and then the spiked samples were tested separately by the approach described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor by combining PCDs and TPPS
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric fluorescent sensor was constructed by combining the green fluorescent PCDs and red fluorescent TPPS. The green fluorescent PCDs were synthesized according to our previously reported method [54,55]. Fig. 1 A depicts the AFM image of fluorescent PCDs, and the AFM analysis suggests that the as-prepared fluorescent PCDs are dispersed homogeneously with a height approximately 1 nm (Fig. 1B). The TEM analysis further confirms that the fluorescent PCDs are successfully prepared, which exhibits a size distribution about 3.5–5.8 nm (Fig. S1). In this work, the green fluorescent PCDs and red fluorescent TPPS were selected to construct the ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the following reasons: Firstly, the fluorescent PCDs and TPPS show excellent water solubility, excitation-independent fluorescence in the excitation wavelength varying from 350 to 450 nm, and the same optimal excitation wavelength (410 nm) which ensures that the PCDs–TPPS hybrid can be excited simultaneously under the optimal excitation wavelength (Fig. 1C and D). Secondly, the maximum emission wavelengths of PCDs (510 nm) and TPPS (644 nm) have a great spectral shift about 135 nm (Fig. 1E), which avoids the severe spectrum overlap for the emission bands of PCDs–TPPS hybrid. As expected, the PCDs–TPPS hybrid exhibits a typical spectrum with two well-resolved fluorescence peaks at 508 and 643 nm at the optimal excitation wavelength (curve c of Fig. 1E). Three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum of the PCDs–TPPS hybrid also confirms that the well-resolved dual-emission fluorescence can be achieved under the optimal excitation wavelength (Fig. 1F). Thirdly, the fluorescence of PCDs can be effectively quenched by TTPS through IEF (Fig. 1E) because its excitation spectrum has a large overlap with the absorption spectrum of TPPS (Fig. S2). Finally, the synthesis of PCDs is simple and TPPS is commercially available. Moreover, there is no need for any covalent linking and surface modification between the PCDs and TPPS, which provides a considerable flexibility and simplicity in the construction of ratiometric fluorescent sensor. More importantly, the TPPS can selectively recognize Hg2+ in the presence of Mn2+, and the PCDs are not responsive to common metal ions (Fig. S3), which endow the as-constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensor with a good selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle and feasibility of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the feasibility of above design and investigate the mechanism of the constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ detection, a series of experiments were performed. As depicted in Fig. S2, the fluorescence excitation spectrum of PCDs overlaps largely with the absorption spectrum of TPPS, while the emission spectrum of PCDs has little overlap with the absorption spectrum of TPPS. Accordingly, the IFE was regarded as the main quenching mechanism between the PCDs and TPPS [56,57]. To confirm this speculation, the fluorescence lifetimes of PCDs were measured in the presence of different TPPS levels. Fig. 2 A exhibits the corresponding fluorescence decay curves of PCDs; it can be seen that the fluorescence lifetime of PCDs is unchanged basically after the addition of TPPS (Table S1). The result confirms that IFE is the dominating quenching mechanism between the PCDs and TPPS. After that, the IFE was corrected by considering of the cuvette geometry (Fig. S4) and the absorption of PCDs and TPPS with Equation S1. The related results are given in Table S2. Fig. 2B depicts the observed and corrected fluorescence quenching efficiency of TPPS toward PCDs, which further suggests that the primary fluorescence quenching of TPPS toward PCDs stems from IFE.

As a commercially available water-soluble porphyrin derivative, TPPS possesses characteristic optical properties such as fascinating fluorescence and high molar absorption coefficient, and plentiful coordination abilities [58,59]. It can chelate with numerous metal ions, while the coordination rate is completely different [60–62]. As far as Mn–TPPS is concerned, the reactive kinetics suggests that the coordination rate of porphyrin and Mn2+ is extremely slow [51,61]. However, Hg2+ can expedite distinctly the complexation of porphyrin and Mn2+. This is because Hg2+ can coordinate quickly with TPPS, which makes the configuration of TPPS beneficial to the complexation of porphyrin and Mn2+ [52,61]. Then, Mn2+ coordinates to the porphyrin ring to form Mn–TPPS, accompanied with the release of Hg2+ [51,52]. The released Hg2+ accelerates repeatedly the complexation of residual TPPS and Mn2+. As shown in Fig. 2C, the individual existence of Mn2+ and Hg2+ cannot cause the fluorescence change of PCDs–TPPS hybrid (curves a, b, and c). However, the coexistence of Mn2+ and Hg2+ triggers a remarkable fluorescence change with fluorescence enhancement at 508 nm and decline at 643 nm for PCDs–TPPS hybrid (curve d of Fig. 2C). The kinetic analysis also proved that Hg2+ can expedite the complexation of TPPS and Mn2+. As depicted in Fig. 2D, the fluorescence of TPPS is unchanged basically when Mn2+ or Hg2+ was added separately to the TPPS solution. However, the fluorescence of TPPS decreases rapidly with the prolongation of time when both Mn2+ and Hg2+ exist in the TPPS solution, revealing that Hg2+ can expedite the complexation of TPPS and Mn2+.

The UV–vis absorption spectra analysis further confirms this principle. Fig. 2E displays the UV–vis absorption spectra of different TPPS solutions, from which it can be seen that there is no notable change for the UV–vis absorption when the Hg2+ or Mn2+ alone exists in TPPS solution (curves a, b, and c). However, the characteristic absorption peaks of TPPS at 413 nm (Soret band) and 515 nm (Q-band) declined remarkably after the addition of both Mn2+ and Hg2+ to the TPPS solution (curve d). Meanwhile, two new absorption peaks appear at about 467 and 560 nm (inset of Fig. 2E), suggesting the formation of manganese porphyrin [51,63,64]. The formation of manganese porphyrin causes a remarkable decrease of the fluorescence and absorption of TPPS, accompanied with the increase of green fluorescence of PCDs (Fig. 2C and E). Meanwhile, a noticeable fluorescence color change from pink to green is observed (inset of Fig. 2C). This is because the Hg2+-triggered formation of manganese porphyrin leads to the decrease of the overlap degree between the absorption spectrum of TPPS and excitation spectrum of PCDs (Fig. 2F), which inhibits the IFE between the TPPS and PCDs and makes the variation of two fluorescence signals possible. The Hg2+-triggered inhibiting effect on IFE caused the change of two fluorescence signals, which provides a possibility to achieve ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: To acquire the best performance for the determination of Hg2+, the sensing conditions were optimized. Initially, the amount of TPPS plays an essential role since the small amount of TPPS will cause a narrow fluorescence color variation, while the excess TPPS will lead to insensitivity of the PCDs–TPPS–Mn2+ sensing system to Hg2+. Therefore, we optimized firstly the amount of TPPS. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity at 508 nm decreases and at 643 nm increases gradually with the increase of TPPS level, accompanied with color change from green to pink. When the TPPS level reached 2.0 μM, the fluorescence intensity and color changed slowly following the increase of TPPS concentration (Fig. 3A and B). Thus, the optimum TPPS concentration was determined to be 2.0 μM. Then, the pH of solution and reaction time was optimized by adding 200 nM Hg2+ to the PCDs–TPPS–Mn2+ sensing system. As depicted in Fig. S5, the sensing system displays the best sensing performance for Hg2+ in pH 7.0 BR buffer. Fig. S6 depicts the time-dependent fluorescence spectra of the sensing system after the addition of Hg2+, which reveals that the reaction reaches equilibrium in 40 min. Therefore, pH 7.0 and 40 min were chosen as the optimum pH value and reaction time, respectively. In addition, the PCDs-TPPS probe also shows a good stability. There is no obvious degradation or aggregation observed after 24 h storage under ambient conditions (Fig. S7).

Under the aforementioned optimal conditions, different levels of Hg2+ were added to the PCDs–TPPS–Mn2+ sensing system to construct the ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform. Fig. 4 A shows the fluorescence spectra of the sensing system in the presence of different levels of Hg2+. The fluorescence intensities at 508 nm increased while those at 643 nm decreased constantly following the increase of Hg2+ concentration (Fig. 4B), accompanied with an obvious fluorescence color change from pink to green (inset in Fig. 4B). A satisfactory linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 643/I 508) and the concentration of Hg2+ can be achieved in the range of 10–200 nM with a regression coefficient of R 2 = 0.995 (Fig. 4C). The regression equation is expressed as follows:
(1)
I 643/I 508 = 1.004 – 0.00395[Hg2+]

The limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ was calculated to be 0.038 nM based on the rule of 3σ (where σ is the standard deviation of 11 blank solutions) [39,40,49–51]. Additionally, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the sensing system also changed obviously after the addition of various levels of Hg2+ due to the formation of manganese porphyrin, which was also applied to detect Hg2+. As depicted in Fig. 4D, the absorbance at 413 nm decreases gradually with increasing Hg2+ concentrations from 0 to 400 nM. The absorbance at 413 nm versus Hg2+ concentration displays a good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.991) from 25 to 200 nM (inset in Fig. 4D). The LOD was 0.050 nM (3σ). The LOD of the proposed ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric methods are lower than the maximum permissible level of Hg2+ in drinking water (10 nM) set by the U.S. EPA [18]. Furthermore, compared to some previously reported approaches for Hg2+ determination (Table S3), our proposed approach not only exhibits high sensitivity toward Hg2+ but also avoids the time-consuming synthesis process and complex surface modification. More significantly, the constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensor shows more remarkable advantages for Hg2+ detection than the single fluorescence signal response, in which only TPPS was used as the probe to detect Hg2+. The single fluorescence signal response can only show a fluorescence brightness variation for Hg2+ (Fig. S8), while the constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensor presents a vivid color change from pink to yellow to green with the increase of Hg2+ concentration (Fig. 4E), which is more easily recognized by the naked eye. These results clearly reveal that the constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensor is more sensitive and reliable for visual sensing of Hg2+ than the single fluorescence response. It provides an effective method for semi-quantitative and visual detection of Hg2+ in on-site and resource-limited settings.

The complex compositions of real water samples pose a great challenge for the developed methods not only in sensitivity but also more importantly in specificity. In order to estimate the specificity of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the determination of Hg2+, some potential interfering substances, including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, As3+, Al3+, Mg2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Cl–, SO4 2–, NO3–, and PO4 3– were added into the sensing system. As depicted in Fig. 5 A and B, only Hg2+ triggers a remarkable fluorescence change even at a lower concentration (200 nM) while other potential interfering ions cause a negligible fluorescence change for the sensing system. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 643/I 508) values of the sensing system for these interferents are similar to that of the blank and remarkably higher than that of Hg2+ (Fig. 5C and D). Although the high concentration of Cu2+ could also cause a change in fluorescence for the sensing system, the Cu2+ induced a negligible fluorescence change at the same concentration level as Hg2+. The above results suggest that the developed approach has a good specificity toward Hg2+ detection. The good specificity of this sensor toward Hg2+ may be attributed to the following two aspects: On the one hand, the fluorescent PCDs are not responsive to common metal ions (Fig. S3), which make that the fluorescence emission peak at 508 nm is not affected by other metal ions. On the other hand, only Hg2+ can induce a remarkable decrease in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the TPPS-Mn2+ (Fig. S9), which inhibits the IFE between the TPPS and PCDs. In other words, only Hg2+ can trigger the inhibition on the IFE between the TPPS and PCDs, leading to the fluorescence recovery of PCDs. As a consequence, the sensor shows a good specificity toward Hg2+. What's more, this high specificity can be visualized by the naked eye under a 365 nm UV lamp (inset in Fig. 5C and D), which provides a simple yet promising method to qualitatively identify Hg2+ in on-site and resource-lacking settings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg2+ in tap water and river water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to validate the feasibility and applicability of the constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the determination of Hg2+ in real samples. We used the developed method to determine Hg2+ in tap water and Jialing River water samples, and there was no Hg2+ detected both in the tap water or Jialing River water (Table S4). Therefore, different concentrations of Hg2+ were added to the tap and river samples to prepare spiked samples and then tested by our developed approach. The related results are presented in Fig. S10 and Table S4. The recoveries of Hg2+ in real water samples obtained by our developed method are 96.2–101.2% and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are between 2.8% and 5.2%, suggesting a good precision and accuracy of the proposed approach. All of these results demonstrated that our developed method holds great potential for Hg2+ detection in real water samples.

In conclusion, we have successfully constructed an effective ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform by a simple mixing of commercially available TPPS and easily synthetic fluorescent PCDs for specific and visual detection of Hg2+. The sensing principle is based on the Hg2+-triggered inhibiting effect on IFE between the TPPS and PCDs. The proposed ratiometric fluorescent sensor not only avoids the time-consuming synthesis process and complex surface modification, but also shows high sensitivity and selectivity toward Hg2+. Moreover, this ratiometric fluorescent sensor was also employed to detect Hg2+ in tap water and river water samples and showed approving results. More significantly, this ratiometric fluorescent sensor shows a vivid color change with the increase of Hg2+ concentration and a distinguishable color variation for Hg2+ over other metal ions, which are readily recognized by the naked eye with the aid of a simple UV lamp. Such a simple and effective ratiometric fluorescent sensor is suitable for not only qualitative identifying of Hg2+ but also semi-quantitative detecting of Hg2+ in on-site and resource-constrained settings.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21273174, 21675131) and the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (No. CSTC-2015jcyjB50001) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2019.05.021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.B. Lu, X.Y. Qin, S. Liu, G.H. Chang, Y.W. Zhang, Y.L. Luo, A.M. Asiri, A.O. Al-Youbi, X.P. Sun, Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions, Anal. Chem. 84 (2012) 5351–5357.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Duplex functional G-quadruplex/NMM fluorescent probe for label-free detection of lead(II) and mercury(II) ions
J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Zhu, L.H. Liu, Y.P. Xing, X.H. Zhou, Duplex functional G-quadruplex/NMM fluorescent probe for label-free detection of lead(II) and mercury(II) ions, J. Hazard. Mater. 355 (2018) 50–55.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. J. Song, M. S. Seo, S. DeBeer George, T. Ohta, R. Song, M.J. Kang, T. Tosha, T. Kitagawa, E.I. Solomon, W. Nam, Synthesis, characterization, and reactivities of manganese (v)−oxo porphyrin complexes, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 129 (2007) 1268–1277.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection and characterization of an oxomanganese (V) porphyrin complex by rapid-mixing stopped-flow spectrophotometry
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.T. Groves, J. Lee, S. S. Marla, Detection and characterization of an oxomanganese (V) porphyrin complex by rapid-mixing stopped-flow spectrophotometry, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 119 (1997) 6269–6273.
